subDescription.accessibilityText: 1 year ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text.text:
#InternshipRecap 🚗
Over the past three months as a Data Engineer Intern at BMW of North America, I’ve had an incredible experience working on the Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS) team. It’s been an exciting opportunity to contribute to projects that focus on the future of automotive technology.

During my internship, I worked on analyzing customer fleet data using PySpark to identify issues and improve next-generation feature packs. I also utilized AWS to run Spark jobs, access data, and perform analytics for developing U.S.-specific requirements. These experiences helped me better understand the role of cloud infrastructure in handling large-scale data processing.

In addition, I assisted in the implementation of the codeBeamer REST API, which enhanced data integration and streamlined workflows for the team. Being involved in these efforts gave me valuable insight into optimizing processes and improving efficiency within a global company like BMW.

On the business side, I had the opportunity to learn about BMW’s overall strategy and gain a deeper understanding of the lifecycle of vehicle development. A highlight for me was exploring the testing and refinement process for BMW's Hands Off Driving feature, which provides Level 2 automonous driving in specific conditions.

It’s been an incredible journey so far, and I’m grateful to Stephan Brinkmann and the ADAS team for all their support throughout my internship. I’ve learned so much about automotive innovation and the importance of rigorous testing in bringing new technology to market. I’m excited to spend the next month with the team and look forward to a strong finish to the year together through the end of December.

#DataEngineering #DataAnalytics #FallIntern #BMW #BMWGroup #AutomotiveTechnology

subDescription.accessibilityText: 6 months ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text.text:
#InternshipRecap 💻
This summer, I had the amazing opportunity to return to Morgan Stanley as a Software Engineer Intern, working on a project to modernize and optimize one of our internal applications. It was an exciting challenge to contribute to software that impacts day-to-day operations at scale.

On the technical side, my work spanned the full stack:
Frontend: Built and modernized UI components using Angular and AG Grid, including new modals and dynamic data formatting, improving usability and aligning with firmwide design standards.
Backend: Developed Spring Boot services to retrieve, add, and save data, integrating multiple SQL stored procedures to replace legacy SOAP functionality.
Database: Converted three SOAP XML processes into efficient SQL stored procedures spanning multiple tables, improving query reliability and reducing load times.
Testing & Deployment: Implemented BDD scenarios and Jest unit tests to ensure reliability, and managed deployments across Dev and QA environments via Jenkins and Grafana.

Beyond the technical work, I enjoyed connecting with other Haitian-American colleagues (shoutout to Christiny Celestin and Abbygaile Jean) at events like the Haitian Power Lunch — a great reminder of the importance of community and culture in the workplace.

A huge thank you to my team and Morgan Stanley for their guidance, collaboration, and code reviews throughout the summer. This experience has strengthened my skills in full-stack development, enterprise testing, and cross-team coordination.
#MorganStanley #InternshipRecap #FullStackDevelopment #Angular #SpringBoot #SQL #TechInternship #SummerIntern

subDescription.accessibilityText: 9 months ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn

text.text:
#InternshipRecap 🎉
I can hardly believe my spring co-op at HubSpot on the CRM Events team just wrapped up after nearly 6 months! As a Software Engineering Intern, I’ve had the incredible opportunity to dive straight into the codebase, collaborate with seasoned engineers, and work on features end-to-end. It’s been an amazing experience from day one.

On the technical side, I worked primarily with TypeScript and React, plus Jinja2 for backend-rendered templates. I shipped 49 merged PRs that included:
- Overhauling UI code to adopt our tokenized design system, cleaning up hundreds of hard-coded styles.
- Building reusable components like an Accordion and a multi-line text wrapper to make event descriptions more maintainable.
- Migrating Events by creating and refining six backend-rendered Jinja2 templates — integrating HubSpot UI pieces while reducing the amount of frontend code.
- Writing 25+ unit tests and 10 end-to-end scenarios to boost reliability and prevent regressions.
- Removing 2,000+ lines of deprecated code (old feature flags, unused styling props) and stripping out legacy test branches.
- Squashing over a dozen UI bugs—fixing icon colors, placeholder logic, edit-mode persistence, and more—so our timelines felt polished and stable.

Beyond the code, a few moments really stood out to me: traveling to Cambridge twice for the Product Summit and Intern Week (first time meeting my team and fellow interns in person!), and co-presenting a Show & Tell on how the Events team is using our new backend-rendered templates to reduce front-end bloat.

I also had the honor of serving as a panelist for the incoming summer class, sharing tips on integrating into HubSpot’s inviting work culture and making an impact from day one.

A huge thank you to my recruiters (Lynn Gomez and Risi Ademola), mentors, and every member of the CRM Events team for your guidance, code reviews, and helpful debugging sessions. I’ve learned so much, and I am so grateful to have worked with you all!

(Special shoutout to the other NJIT HubSpotters - Dylan Ton-That + Rodney Nuñez 🐐)
